--- SOURCE 1: CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing for neurodegenerative diseases - PMC ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/

SUMMARY:
Among these tools, CRISPR-Cas-based gene editing stands out because of its ability to introduce heritable genome changes by designing short guide RNAs. This review aims to provide an overview of CRISPR-Cas technology and summarizes the latest research on the application of CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing technology for the treatment of the most prevalent neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and Spinocerebellar ataxia. Consequently, CRISPR/Cas9 has evolved into an uncomplicated and adaptable RNA-directed system for genome editing that can be used on a variety of organisms and cell types, ranging from bacteria and mice to rats, zebrafish, pigs, human somatic cells, and human pluripotent stem cells for treating human diseases (Cho et al., 2013[14]; Henao-Mejia et al., 2016[33]; Mali et al., 2013[61]; Tu et al., 2015[95]; Vejnar et al., 2016[97]).

Abstract
--------

Gene therapy has emerged as a promising therapeutic strategy for various conditions, including blood disorders, ocular disease, cancer, and nervous system disorders. The advent of gene editing techniques has facilitated the ability of researchers to specifically target and modify the eukaryotic cell genome, making it a valuable tool for gene therapy. This can be performed through either _in vivo_ or _ex vivo_ approaches. Gene editing tools, such as zinc finger nucleases, transcription activator-like effector nucleases, and CRISPR-Cas-associated nucleases, can be employed for gene therapy purposes. Among these tools, CRISPR-Cas-based gene editing stands out because of its ability to introduce heritable genome changes by designing short guide RNAs. This review aims to provide an overview of CRISPR-Cas technology and summarizes the latest research on the application of CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing technology for the treatment of the most prevalent neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and Spinocerebellar ataxia.

**Keywords:** gene editing, neurodegenerative disorders, CRISPR/Cas9, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, Spinocerebellar ataxia

Introduction
------------

Gene therapy is a promising alternative therapeutic strategy for a variety of conditions, including blood disorders, ocular disease, cancer, and nervous system disorders. The advent of gene editing techniques has facilitated the ability of researchers to specifically and selectively target and modify the eukaryotic cell genome, making it a valuable tool for gene therapy (Gonçalves and Paiva, 2017[[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R26)]). Genome editing techniques involve breaking the DNA double-strand (DSBs) sequence at specific locations in the genome, which can be repaired using different cellular DNA repair mechanisms, such as endogenous homology-directed repair (HDR), microhomology-mediated end joining (MMEJ), classical non-homologous end joining (cNHEJ), and single-strand annealing (SSA) pathways (Zhao and Hu, 2020[[113](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R113)]). Gene therapy can be performed through either _in vivo_ or _ex vivo_ approaches (Parambi et al., 2022[[74](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R74)]). The _in vivo_ strategy involves delivering genetic material directly to a specific tissue in the patient, while the _ex vivo_ strategy involves modifying cells _in vitro_ and then transplanting them back into the patient (Gowing et al., 2017[[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R28)]; Naldini, 2011[[65](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R65)]). Gene editing tools, including zinc finger nucleases (ZFN), transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALEN), and clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat (CRISPR)-Cas-associated nucleases, can be employed for gene therapy purposes (Zhao and Hu, 2020[[113](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R113)]). Although each of these gene editing tools has its own advantages, CRISPR-Cas-based gene editing stands out because of its ability to introduce heritable genome changes by designing short guide RNAs for protein-coding genes, rather than using modified proteins, which was one of the main limitations of ZFN and TALEN (Eid and Mahfouz, 2016[[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R21)]). The utilization of the CRISPR/Cas9 system for genome editing is a highly effective approach that presents a superior alternative to conventional methods, offering potential advantages for gene therapy (Gopinath et al., 2015[[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R27)]). With its capability to delete DNA sequences and correct mutations, CRISPR/Cas9 provides a novel pathway for treating diseases (Ran et al., 2013[[80](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R80)]). This review aims to provide a brief overview of CRISPR-Cas technology, followed by a comprehensive summary of the latest research on the application of CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing technology for the treatment of various neurodegenerative diseases.

### CRISPR-Cas gene editing system

Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats (CRISPR) were first discovered in Escherichia coli in 1987 (Ishino et al., 1987[[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R34)]). Concurrently, several genes that were closely linked to CRISPR and identified as CRISPR-associated (Cas) genes were also identified (Jansen et al., 2002[[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R36)]; Makarova and Koonin, 2007[[58](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R58)]). It has been established that CRISPR and RNA-guided Cas proteins work together (Makarova et al., 2006[[56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R56)]), and the functionality of CRISPR-Cas as an adaptive immune system of prokaryotic cells to defend against invading phages was fully validated by Barrangou's lab in 2007 (Barrangou et al., 2007[[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R4)]).

The immune response of CRISPR-Cas has three primary stages: adaptation, expression, and interference (Makarova et al., 2011[[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R57)]). During the adaptation phase, a complex of Cas proteins binds to a specific region of DNA, often marked by a short motif known as PAM, and removes a section of the DNA called protospacer. The adaptation complex then inserts the protospacer DNA into the CRISPR array as a spacer, either by duplicating the repeat at the 5' end or by acquiring it from RNA via reverse transcription. In the expression stage, the CRISPR array is transcribed into pre-CRISPR RNA (pre-crRNA) and processed into mature CRISPR RNAs (crRNAs), with each one of them consists of the spacer sequence and certain parts of the flanking repeats. This processing is mediated by different subunits of a Cas complex, a single multidomain Cas protein, or non-Cas host RNases, depending on the CRISPR-Cas variant. During the interference phase, the crRNA remains bound to the processing complex and guides the Cas nuclease to the protospacer or a similar sequence in the genome of a virus or plasmid that is subsequently cleaved and inactivated (Faure et al., 2019[[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R22)]; Jiang and Doudna, 2017[[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R38)]; Koonin and Makarova, 2019[[48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R48)]; Makarova et al., 2020[[60](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R60)]; McGinn and Marraffini, 2019[[62](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R62)]).

The CRISPR-Cas systems can be categorized into two classes based on their effector module design principles. These classes consist of six types (I to VI) and have several subtypes. The Class 1 systems (Type I, III, and IV) contain multi-Cas protein effector complexes, whereas Class 2 systems (Type II, V, and VI) have a single effector protein (Koonin and Makarova, 2019[[48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R48)]; Makarova et al., 2015[[59](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R59)]). Class 1 CRISPR-Cas systems contain various Cas proteins in their effector module, including Cas3 (sometimes connected to Cas2), Cas5-Cas8, Cas10, and Cas11. The combination of these proteins depends on the system's type and subtype. On the other hand, in class 2 systems, a solitary, significant protein, such as Cas9, Cas12, or Cas13, constitutes the effector module (Makarova et al., 2020[[60](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R60)]).

### CRISPR-Cas9 system

The Type II CRISPR-Cas9 system, which is derived from Streptococcus pyogenes (SpyCas9), is extensively studied and widely employed among various CRISPR-Cas systems for genome modification (Jiang and Doudna, 2017[[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R38)]). SpyCas9 is dependent on the presence of two RNA molecules, a crRNA, and a trans-activating crRNA (tracrRNA), to cleave DNA. However, these two RNAs can be combined into a single guide RNA (sgRNA) without altering their individual abilities (Jinek et al., 2012[[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R42)]). Briefly, CRISPR-Cas9 system primarily consists of an RNA-guided Cas9 endonuclease and an sgRNA to direct the Cas9 nuclease towards specific location within the genomic DNA sequences (Mali et al., 2013[[61](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R61)]; Ran et al., 2013[[80](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R80)]; Tu et al., 2015[[95](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R95)]). This results in the creation of a double-stranded break (DSB), which can be fixed by two endogenous self-repair mechanisms found within the organism: the non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) pathway or the homology-directed repair (HDR) pathway (Figure 1[(Fig. 1)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#F1)) (Xu and Li, 2020[[105](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R105)]).

#### Figure 1. The schematic diagram of CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing illustrates how the Cas9 nuclease forms complexes with sgRNA, enabling the production of specific double-strand breaks (DSBs) in the host DNA. These targeted DNA DSBs can activate the endogenous cellular repair machinery. The DSBs are subsequently repaired through two main mechanisms. Firstly, homology-directed repair (HDR) utilizes the sister chromatid as a template DNA, facilitating precise genomic changes in the host's DNA. Secondly, nonhomologous end joining (NHEJ) occurs when DNA ends are joined without any homology, leading to the introduction of insertions and deletions.

[![Image 9: Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/90ac/10450213/fd9897fb1490/EXCLI-22-567-g-001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10450213_EXCLI-22-567-g-001.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/figure/F1/)

The Cas9 protein is a complex structure consisting of multiple domains, with two distinct parts referred to as the nuclease (NUC) lobe and the recognition (REC) lobe. These two lobes are linked by a bridge helix (BH) containing a high concentration of arginine amino acids (Anders et al., 2014[[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R2)]; Babu et al., 2021[[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R3)]; Jiang et al., 2016[[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R39)]; Murugan et al., 2017[[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R64)]; Yamada et al., 2017[[106](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R106)]; Zhang et al., 2020[[112](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R112)]). The NUC lobe of Cas9 encompasses two endonuclease domains called HNH and RuvC, each of which cuts one strand of the double-stranded DNA being targeted, as well as a PAM-interacting domain to enable the Cas9 protein to bind to the target DNA (Anders et al., 2014[[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R2)]; Babu et al., 2021[[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R3)]). The Cas9 nuclease and sgRNA form a Cas9 ribonucleoprotein (RNP) capable of binding to and cleaving the specific DNA target (Jinek et al., 2012[[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R41)]). In addition, the REC lobe of SpCas9 is composed of several recognition domains (REC1-REC3), which aid in the attachment of SpyCas9 to RNA and DNA (Figure 2[(Fig. 2)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#F2)) (Babu et al., 2021[[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R3)]; Jiang and Doudna, 2017[[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R38)]; Jiang et al., 2015[[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R40)]; Yamada et al., 2017[[106](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R106)]).

#### Figure 2. Schematic illustrations demonstrate the proposed mechanisms involved in recognizing and cleaving target DNA using CRISPR-Cas9. When the sgRNA is loaded, Cas9 undergoes a significant rearrangement to enter a target-recognition mode. In this mode, the PAM-interacting cleft (represented by a dotted circle), which is disordered in apo-Cas9, becomes prestructured for PAM sampling. Additionally, the guide RNA seed adopts an A-form helical conformation, facilitating the interrogation of adjacent DNA for guide RNA complementarity. The disordered nonseed RNA nucleotides are represented by dotted white boxes. Further activation of Cas9 occurs through a series of coordinated steps. These steps include PAM recognition, local DNA melting, RNA strand invasion, and stepwise R-loop formation. Concomitantly, the conformational change of the HNH domain leads to allosteric regulation of the RuvC domain, ensuring concerted DNA cleavage. Abbreviations: REC (recognition lobe), NUC (nuclease lobe), PAM (protospacer adjacent motif), bp (base pair), and sgRNA (single-guide RNA) (Jiang and Doudna, 2017).

[![Image 10: Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/90ac/10450213/c0ad38cd3972/EXCLI-22-567-g-002.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10450213_EXCLI-22-567-g-002.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/figure/F2/)

Cas9's reliable and robust function has made it suitable as a programmable tool for generating DNA double-strand breaks both _in vitro_ and _in vivo_ (Fellmann et al., 2017[[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R23)]; Mali et al., 2013[[61](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R61)]; Oakes et al., 2019[[68](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R68)]). Consequently, CRISPR/Cas9 has evolved into an uncomplicated and adaptable RNA-directed system for genome editing that can be used on a variety of organisms and cell types, ranging from bacteria and mice to rats, zebrafish, pigs, human somatic cells, and human pluripotent stem cells for treating human diseases (Cho et al., 2013[[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R14)]; Henao-Mejia et al., 2016[[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R33)]; Mali et al., 2013[[61](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R61)]; Tu et al., 2015[[95](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R95)]; Vejnar et al., 2016[[97](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R97)]). In 2016, the first instance of CRISPR/Cas9 being used in a clinical setting took place. A group from China administered CRISPR/ Cas9-edited cells to patients with advanced lung cancer as a treatment (Cyranoski, 2016[[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R17)]). Over the past few years, numerous clinical trials have been conducted, and several findings have been published. These findings involve the use of CRISPR/Cas9-based treatments for conditions such as acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS), sickle cell disease (SCD), β-thalassemia, and multiple types of cancers (Guo et al., 2022[[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R29)]).

### Delivery system of CRISPR-Cas9

To modify genes in mammalian cells for therapeutic purposes, the CRISPR components need to be delivered into the cells. There are different approaches to deliver the CRISPR/Cas9 components, including viral, mRNA, plasmid, and protein-based methods (Lu et al., 2021[[52](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R52)]). Among these methods, viral delivery is the most commonly used for CRISPR/Cas9. Various virus delivery systems, such as adeno-associated viruses (AAV), Adenoviral Vectors (AdV), and Lentiviral Vectors (LV), have been utilized for this purpose (Xu et al., 2019[[104](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R104)]). AAVs are extensively used in CRISPR genome editing for the following reasons: First, AAVs can penetrate the host cell and persist independently from the host cell genome, allowing the provirus to express continuously and stably for up to 1-2 years, which is advantageous for treating diseases. Second, AAVs can infect a variety of tissues owing to their diverse capsids (Weinmann et al., 2020[[101](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R101)]). Third, AAVs are capable of withstanding changes in pH and temperature while maintaining their stable activity (Rayaprolu et al., 2013[[82](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R82)]).

Another commonly used method for introducing CRISPR technology into cells is by delivering Cas9-encoded mRNA. mRNA-based approaches have a temporary function and avoid the potential risks linked to integrating into the host genome (Nelles et al., 2016[[67](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R67)]). Additionally, these strategies have the advantage of faster results, as mRNA can be transcribed within minutes (Polstein and Gersbach, 2015[[78](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R78)]).

Plasmid-based approaches are also an attractive method for delivering CRISPR components into cells. This approach offers several benefits. First, gene synthesis is a straightforward process. Second, the created gene can be transferred to the host cell through a plasmid without requiring integration into the host genome, allowing for continuous expression. Additionally, targeted delivery of the CRISPR/Cas9 system to specific organs is crucial for its future applications. Plasmid-based delivery has the potential advantage of enabling tissue or cell-specific targeting by integrating it directly into the plasmid (Lu et al., 2021[[52](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R52)]).

Another effective method for delivering CRISPR components is Protein-based CRISPR/Cas9 strategies, which have revolutionized genetic engineering. In this system, Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) is an essential component of the CRISPR/Cas9 system. In CRISPR-Cas9 RNP delivery system, the Cas9 protein is delivered to the target cells as an RNP complex, which consists of the Cas9 protein and an sgRNA. The RNP complex offers several advantages, including higher specificity, reduced off-target effects, and increased efficiency, making it the attractive method of delivery (Kang et al., 2019[[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R43)]; Kim et al., 2014[[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R45)]). The Cas9 ribonucleoprotein (RNP) approach is versatile and adaptable to different types of model organisms and cells, including immune cells, primary cells, and stem cells (Schumann et al., 2015[[84](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R84)]; Seki and Rutz, 2018[[85](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R85)]; Wang et al., 2021[[100](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R100)]).

### CRISPR-Cas9 in neurodegenerative diseases

Neurodegeneration refers to the gradual decline of the structure and function of neurons, resulting in the loss of these neural cells. As time goes on, this deterioration progressively worsens and can ultimately lead to nervous system dysfunction (Kovacs, 2019[[49](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R49)]). The group of diseases that are characterized by neurodegeneration are collectively referred to as neurodegenerative diseases (NDs). The most prevalent NDs include Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and Spinocerebellar ataxia (Lamptey et al., 2022[[50](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R50)]). The CRISPR-Cas9 system has demonstrated promising results in the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases. In the subsequent sections, we will describe the applications of CRISPR-Cas9 that have been employed for these disorders.

### Application of CRISPR/Cas9 in Alzheimer's disease

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a prevalent progressive neurodegenerative disorder that affects millions of people globally, currently ranking as the third leading cause of death following heart disease and cancer (Rohn et al., 2018[[83](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R83)]). The disease is often characterized by memory deficits and cognitive decline, which eventually impacts memory, language, behavior, movement, judgment, and reasoning, leading to dementia and eventual death (Jha et al., 2020[[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R37)]; Parambi et al., 2022[[74](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R74)]). AD is mainly characterized by two neuropathological features, the accumulation of extracellular amyloid plaques containing amyloid β-protein (Aβ) and neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) primarily composed of hyperphosphorylated Tau protein, a microtubule-associated protein. The relationship between these two pathologies is considered the classical hallmark of AD (Šimić et al., 2016[[88](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R88)]; Yan and Vassar, 2014[[107](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R107)]). The classical β-amyloid hypothesis provided a framework for the development of potential disease-modifying therapies that would prevent Aβ formation and promote the elimination of toxic proteins (such as Aβ) from the brain (Rohn et al., 2018[[83](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R83)]). However, despite numerous attempts to develop disease-modifying treatments using animal models of the disease, there have been many failures (Cummings et al., 2014[[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R16)]; Galimberti and Scarpini, 2011[[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R25)]). Consequently, over the past few years, the CRISPR/Cas9 technology has gained popularity in the field of Alzheimer's disease due to its short experimental duration and low consumption. It is currently being extensively utilized for tasks such as building AD models, identifying pathogenic genes through screening, and for targeted therapy (Lu et al., 2021[[52](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R52)]).

Although the majority of cases of AD are sporadic, a small percentage of cases are familial (known as Familial AD or FAD), caused by dominant autosomal mutations found in one of three genes: amyloid precursor protein (APP), presenilin-1 (PSEN1), and presenilin-2 (PSEN2) (Bertram and Tanzi, 2012[[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R6)]; Zhang et al., 2020[[111](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R111)]). PSEN1 mutations are the primary reason behind familial AD and typically result in an earlier onset of symptoms when compared to mutations found in the other two genes. The majority of PSEN1 mutations result in an elevated production of the more aggregation-prone Aβ42 compared to Aβ40 (Shea et al., 2016[[86](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R86)]). This abnormal production of Aβ42 is known to contribute to the formation of Aβ plaques in the brain, which is a hallmark of Alzheimer's disease. Recent studies indicate that the CRISPR/Cas9 technique has the potential to effectively rectify autosomal dominant mutations. This gene editing system has been reported to successfully correct similar types of mutations, further reinforcing its potential for genetic modification.

A study conducted in 2022 by Konstantinidis et al. suggests that the CRISPR-Cas9 approach can selectively disrupt the PSEN1M146L allele responsible for AD, and partially reverse the abnormal Aβ42/40 ratio that leads to the development of the disease in carriers of this mutation (Konstantinidis et al., 2022[[47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10450213/#R47)]). Furthermore, another study has demonstrated that using CRISPR-Cas9 to correct neurons derived from the PSEN2N141I mutated individual fibroblasts can normalize the Aβ42/40 ratio and effectively restore the associated electrophysiological

Abstract
--------

The [clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/crispr "Learn more about clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CRISPR)/CRISPR-associated protein 9 (Cas9) system is an acquired immune system of many bacteria and [archaea](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/archaeon "Learn more about archaea from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), comprising _CRISPR_ loci, _Cas_ genes, and its associated proteins. This system can recognize [exogenous DNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/exogenous-dna "Learn more about exogenous DNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and utilize the Cas9 protein's [nuclease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/nuclease "Learn more about nuclease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") activity to break DNA double-strand and to achieve [base](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/base "Learn more about base from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") insertion or deletion by subsequent [DNA repair](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-repair "Learn more about DNA repair from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In recent years, multiple laboratory and clinical studies have revealed the therapeutic role of the CRISPR/Cas9 system in [neurological diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurologic-disease "Learn more about neurological diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This article reviews the CRISPR/Cas9-mediated gene editing technology and its potential for clinical application against [neurological diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/nervous-system-disorder "Learn more about neurological diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Introduction
------------

CRISPR/Cas9 system belongs to the third generation of gene editing technology after [zinc finger nucleases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/zinc-finger-nuclease "Learn more about zinc finger nucleases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), transcription activation-like effect [nucleases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nuclease "Learn more about nucleases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and other techniques.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib1)CRISPR/Cas9 become the focus of current research with its simple and efficient features. This system allows DNA double-strand to break at corresponding targets by the nuclease activity of [Cas9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/crispr-associated-protein "Learn more about Cas9 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") proteins.[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib2)Multiple gene editing techniques have been developed based on this principle, including gene knock-out, gene knock-in, transcriptional regulation, and [base](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/base "Learn more about base from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") editing.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib3), [4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib4), [5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib5), [6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib6)The basic principle of the CRISPR/Cas9 system is to identify the target gene by an artificially designed single [guide RNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/guide-rna "Learn more about guide RNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (sgRNA). The sgRNA guides the cleavage of DNA double strands by [Cas9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/crispr-associated-protein "Learn more about Cas9 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and forms double-strand breaks (DSB) at the target site, after the DNA damage spontaneous repair occurs, during which gene knock-out or knock-in can be achieved.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib7)

[Nervous system diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurologic-disease "Learn more about Nervous system diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have complex etiology, are relatively difficult to treat, and seriously affect patients' [quality of life](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/quality-of-life "Learn more about quality of life from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). WHO predicts that by 2050, [neurodegenerative diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/degenerative-disease "Learn more about neurodegenerative diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") will replace cancer as the leading cause of death in humans.[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib8)The CRISPR/Cas9 gene-editing technology can effectively treat neurological diseases, as confirmed by recent studies demonstrating the therapeutic value of the CRISPR/Cas9 system in [Alzheimer's disease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/alzheimers-disease "Learn more about Alzheimer's disease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AD), [Parkinson's disease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/parkinsons-disease "Learn more about Parkinson's disease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PD), [Huntington's disease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/huntingtons-disease "Learn more about Huntington's disease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HD), [glioblastoma](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/glioblastoma "Learn more about glioblastoma from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (GBM), epilepsy, [amyotrophic lateral sclerosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/amyotrophic-lateral-sclerosis "Learn more about amyotrophic lateral sclerosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ALS), _etc_.[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib9), [10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib10), [11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib11), [12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib12), This review expounds on the mechanism of the CRISPR/Cas9 system and its mediated gene editing technology ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#fig1)). It summarizes its research progress associated with the pathogenesis and treatment of nervous system diseases and will provide new ideas for its prevention and treatment ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#tbl1)).

Structure of CRISPR/Cas9 system
-------------------------------

### _CRISPR_ gene

The [CRISPR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/crispr "Learn more about CRISPR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") gene is a repetitive sequence in the [prokaryotic genome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/microbial-genome "Learn more about prokaryotic genome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that is distributed in 40% of sequenced bacteria and 90% of sequenced [archaea](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/archaeon "Learn more about archaea from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib15)_[CRISPR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/clustered-regularly-interspaced-short-palindromic-repeat "Learn more about CRISPR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ gene sequence mainly comprises leader, repeat, and spacer.[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib16), [17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib17), [18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib18)The [leader sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/signal-peptide "Learn more about leader sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is located upstream of the _CRISPR_ gene, is relatively simple with high AT content, and acts as a promoter. The repeats are a [palindromic sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/palindromic-sequence "Learn more about palindromic sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of about 20–50 bp long. The transcripts of the repeats form hairpin-like structures essential for maintaining [RNA structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rna-structure "Learn more about RNA structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and functional integrity. The spacer sequence is an exogenous [DNA sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/dna-sequence "Learn more about DNA sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") captured by bacteria, making it the product of bacterial adaptive immunity. When the bacteria detect these [exogenous DNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/exogenous-dna "Learn more about exogenous DNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") sequences, the CRISPR/Cas system acts as a part of the immune system.

### _Cas_ gene and Cas9 protein

The _Cas_ gene is located adjacent to the _CRISPR_ gene, but a portion of the _Cas_ is also discretely distributed elsewhere in the genome. Cas, the encoded product of the _Cas_ gene, is a protein with [nuclease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/nuclease "Learn more about nuclease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") activity and is a key component in bacterial autonomous immunity that binds to DNA at its corresponding targets. Based on the different roles of Cas in the immune mechanism, CRISPR/Cas system is divided into two categories. In the first category, Cas (type I, III, and IV) act through a cascade amplification mechanism, which usually produces the Cas complex. In the second category, Cas does not rely on a cascade amplification mechanism, and only a single Cas is involved, including type II and type V. [Cas9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/crispr-associated-protein "Learn more about Cas9 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") belongs to type II.[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib19)

Mechanism of CRISPR/Cas9 system
-------------------------------

### Adaptation

The first step is “adaptation”, which involves the acquisition of a spacer sequence. Phage or plasmid-derived [exogenous DNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/exogenous-dna "Learn more about exogenous DNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is integrated into the phosphate group of the _CRISPR_ gene when bacteria are attacked.[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib20)Therefore, it can be speculated that the position of the phosphate group end to the [base](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/base "Learn more about base from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") end in the spacer sequence is consistent with the temporal order of exogenous DNA invasion. [Protospacer adjacent motif](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protospacer-adjacent-motif "Learn more about Protospacer adjacent motif from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PAM) is a hallmark of the pre-spacer sequence and can be used to distinguish exogenous DNA and _CRISPR_ genes.[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib21)Cas1 and Cas2 are the critical proteins that take part in the adaptation stage. To perform their function, both proteins usually polymerize to form a Cas1-Cas2 complex.[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib22),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib23)The Cas1-Cas2 complex cleaves the [DNA sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-sequence "Learn more about DNA sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") near PAM by utilizing it as the recognition object. The original spacer sequence is then inserted downstream of the [leader sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/signal-peptide "Learn more about leader sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and finally, the DNA completes the spontaneous repair. During the adaptation phase, bacteria generate specific immunity against the exogenous DNA, therefore when it encounters invaders containing the same [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") material again; it specifically identifies and destroys these invaders.

### Expression

The [leader sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/signal-peptide "Learn more about leader sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") contains the promoter and spacer sequence integration elements of the CRISPR/Cas9 system. When the intruder is identified, the [leader sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/signal-peptide "Learn more about leader sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") regulates the _CRISPR_[gene transcription](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetic-transcription "Learn more about gene transcription from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to synthesize base-complementary pre-CRISPR [RNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rna "Learn more about RNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (pre-crRNA) and trans-activating crRNA (tracrRNA).[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib24)Pre-crRNA, [tracrRNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/trans-activating-crrna "Learn more about tracrRNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and Cas9 constitute a complex. Subsequently, the pre-crRNA is sliced into smaller units by [RNase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/ribonuclease "Learn more about RNase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") III, namely CRISPR RNA (crRNA). crRNA corresponds to the corresponding spacer sequence and guides Cas9 to target the [DNA cleavage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/dna-cleavage "Learn more about DNA cleavage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") site.[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib25)crRNA-tracrRNA-Cas9 triplet complex is the basic structural and functional unit of the CRISPR/Cas9 system.[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib26)

### Interference

PAM of exogenous DNA is first recognized by the Cas1-Cas2 complex. Subsequently, crRNA further guides the binding of the crRNA-tracrRNA-Cas9 triple complex to this exogenous DNA according to the principle of base complementary pairing. The crRNA then cooperates with a matching complement (protospacer) on an invading DNA to form the R-loop.[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib27)In the R-loop structure, crRNA binds to one strand by complementary base pairing, and the other single strand maintains a free state. At this time, Cas9 exerts its [endonuclease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/endonuclease "Learn more about endonuclease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") activity, generates a target cleavage site 3 nucleotides upstream of PAM, and forms a DSB, thereby eliminating the exogenous after a series of complex actions.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib7)The six domains of Cas9 have different mechanisms and functions. The NHN domain and the RuvC domain are responsible for targeted [DNA cleavage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/dna-cleavage "Learn more about DNA cleavage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The NHN domain cuts the complementary [DNA strands](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-strand "Learn more about DNA strands from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), regulated by single [metal ions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/metal-ion "Learn more about metal ions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the RuvC domain cuts the non-complementary [DNA strands](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/dna-strand "Learn more about DNA strands from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), regulated by bimetallic ions.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib7),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib28)

Gene editing technology based on CRISPR/Cas9 system
---------------------------------------------------

The [sgRNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/guide-rna "Learn more about sgRNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a synthetic T-shaped [RNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rna "Learn more about RNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that guides Cas9 to bind the target DNA and can achieve editing for different genes instead of the crRNA.[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib29),[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352304223001459#bib30)The basic mechanism of the CRISPR/Cas9 system involves guiding Cas9 to cleave [DNA double strands](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/double-stranded-dna "Learn more about DNA double strands from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") after [sgRNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/guide-rna "Learn more about sgRNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") recognizes the target gene and forms DSB at the corresponding site.                                                     <p>CRISPR genome editing strategy. At the top, the Cas9 protein, guided by a single guide RNA (sgRNA, shown in pink), binds to a complementary target DNA sequence adjacent to a PAM (Protospacer Adjacent Motif) sequence (shown in blue). Upon binding, Cas9 introduces a site-specific double-stranded break in the DNA. Following the DSB, the cell can repair the damage via two main pathways: (I) Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ): this error-prone repair mechanism ligates the broken DNA ends without a template, often resulting in small insertions or deletions (indels). These indels can disrupt the reading frame of the gene, leading to disrupted or nonfunctional DNA. The figure illustrates two outcomes: nucleotide deletion leading to disrupted DNA, and nucleotide addition (or random insertions), also resulting in disrupted DNA. (II) Homology-Directed Repair (HDR): this high-fidelity repair mechanism uses a homologous DNA template (donor DNA, shown in pink) to repair the break precisely. HDR enables the correction or replacement of a specific gene of interest, resulting in accurately repaired DNA.</p>

                                                    <p>CRISPR-based gene expression regulation. The Transcriptional Activation System used dCas9 (a “dead” Cas9 lacking nuclease activity) guided by a single-guide RNA (sgRNA) to a specific DNA sequence near the promoter of a target gene. A transcriptional activator is fused to the dCas9 protein or recruited via the sgRNA scaffold. Finally, the dCas9-activator complex binds upstream of the transcription start site (TSS), facilitating increased transcription of the target gene. This approach is commonly used in CRISPRa (CRISPR activation) to upregulate gene expression without altering the genomic sequence. The transcriptional repression system, referred to as CRISPRi (CRISPR interference), uses dCas9 bound to a target sequence near the promoter region, guided by an sgRNA. In this case, dCas9 is fused to a KRAB (Krüppel-associated box) transcriptional repressor domain. The KRAB-dCas9 complex blocks transcription initiation or recruits chromatin-modifying factors that lead to gene silencing.</p>

Abstract
--------

Neurodegenerative diseases (NDs) pose a major challenge to global healthcare systems owing to their devastating effects and limited treatment options. These disorders are characterized by progressive loss of neuronal structure and function, resulting in cognitive and motor impairments. Current therapies primarily focus on symptom management rather than on targeting the underlying causes. However, clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat (CRISPR) technology offers a promising alternative by enabling precise genetic modifications that could halt or even reverse ND progression. CRISPR-Cas9, the most widely used CRISPR system, acts as a molecular scissor targeting specific DNA sequences for editing. By designing guide RNAs (gRNAs) to match sequences in genes associated with NDs, researchers can leverage CRISPR to knockout harmful genes, correct mutations, or insert protective genes. This review explores the potential of CRISPR-based therapies in comparison with traditional treatments for NDs. As research advances, CRISPR has the potential to revolutionize ND treatment by addressing its genetic underpinnings. Ongoing clinical trials and preclinical studies continue to expand our understanding and application of this powerful tool to fight debilitating conditions.

1. Introduction
---------------

Neurodegenerative diseases (NDs) such as Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Parkinson’s disease (PD), Huntington’s disease (HD), and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) are a growing public health concern. These disorders result from gradual deterioration of the nervous system, leading to cognitive impairment and motor dysfunction [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4425/16/8/850#B1-genes-16-00850)]. As the global population ages, the prevalence and impact of NDs are expected to rise significantly, imposing substantial burdens on individuals, families, healthcare systems, and economies [[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4425/16/8/850#B2-genes-16-00850)]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop more effective treatment strategies. Current therapeutic approaches primarily focus on symptomatic relief and slowing disease progression but fail to address the underlying causes [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4425/16/8/850#B3-genes-16-00850)]. For instance, HD and ALS rely on symptomatic treatments such as tetrabenazine for chorea and riluzole for ALS, both of which offer neuroprotective benefits. However, these treatments are limited by their inability to halt or reverse neuronal degeneration. Moreover, their efficacy often diminishes over time, and adverse side effects frequently accompany their use. The absence of disease-modifying therapies highlights a critical gap in ND management [[4](https:/

SUMMARY:
This review explores the role of CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing in the management of AD and its clinical manifestations. Therefore, the purpose of this review was to examine the potential utility of CRISPR/Cas9 as a treatment option for AD by targeting specific genes, including those that cause early onset AD, as well as those that are significant risk factors for late-onset AD such as the apolipoprotein E4 (APOE4) gene. However, the present review mainly elaborates on the possible role of CRISPR/Cas9 in AD due to its low cost, high speed, efficiency, and precision over other genome editing tools. Dr. Kamal Dua, Lecturer, Discipline of Pharmacy, Graduate School of Health, University of Technology Sydney (UTS), has a research experience of over 12 years working in the field of drug delivery targeting inflammatory diseases.

Highlights
----------

*   •Mutations in APP, PSEN1 and PSEN2 are known factors for AD pathobiology. 
*   •CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing approach hold promises in AD management. 
*   •CRISPR/Cas9 is utilized to help correct anomalous [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") functions. 
*   •Off-target mutations may impair the functionality of edited cells. 
*   •Non-viral vectors show better efficacy and safety than viral vectors. 

Abstract
--------

### Background

[Alzheimer's disease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/alzheimers-disease "Learn more about Alzheimer's disease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AD) is an insidious, irreversible, and progressive neurodegenerative health condition manifesting as [cognitive deficits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cognitive-defect "Learn more about cognitive deficits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [amyloid beta](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amyloid-beta "Learn more about amyloid beta from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Aβ) plaques and [neurofibrillary tangles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/neurofibrillary-tangle "Learn more about neurofibrillary tangles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Approximately 50 million individuals are affected by AD, and the number is rapidly increasing globally. This review explores the role of CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing in the management of AD and its clinical manifestations.

### Aim of Review

This review aims to provide a deep insight into the recent progress in CRISPR/Cas9-mediated genome editing and its use against [neurodegenerative disorders](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurodegenerative-disorder "Learn more about neurodegenerative disorders from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), specifically AD. However, we have referred to its use against parkinsons’s disease (PD), Huntington’s disease (HD), and other human diseases, as is one of the most promising and emerging technologies for disease treatment.

### Key Scientific Concepts of Review

The [pathophysiology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pathophysiology "Learn more about pathophysiology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of AD is known to be linked with [gene mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/gene-mutation "Learn more about gene mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), that is, [presenilin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/presenilin "Learn more about presenilin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PSEN) and [amyloid beta precursor protein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/amyloid-precursor-protein "Learn more about amyloid beta precursor protein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (APP). However, [clinical trials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/clinical-trial "Learn more about clinical trials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") focused at the genetic level could not meet the desired efficiency. The CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing tool is one of the most powerful technologies for correcting inconsistent genetic signatures and now extensively used for AD management. It has significant potential for the correction of undesired [gene mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-mutation "Learn more about gene mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") associated with AD. This technology has allowed the development of empirical AD models, therapeutic lines, and diagnostic approaches for better understanding the nervous system, from _in vitro_ to _in vivo_ models.

Introduction
------------

Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a major worldwide health concern with enormous social and economic impact [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0005). The slow decline in cognitive function and irreversible neuronal loss are the primary etiological manifestations of the disease. AD is a chronic neurodegenerative condition in which cognition and memory formation gradually deteriorate due to an irreversible neuron loss. It is characterized by the formation and accumulation of amyloid-beta 42 (Aβ42) and phosphorylated Tau, along with excessive [glial cell](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/glial-cells "Learn more about glial cell from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") activation. Further, impaired synaptic function and insufficient [neurotrophin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/neurotrophin "Learn more about neurotrophin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") signaling are important characteristics of AD. Primary symptoms include [memory loss](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/lacunar-amnesia "Learn more about memory loss from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), apathy, depression, and irritability [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0010). Despite substantial researches, the etiology, [pathophysiology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pathophysiology "Learn more about pathophysiology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and mechanisms of both [cognitive impairment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cognitive-defect "Learn more about cognitive impairment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and synaptic dysfunction are not well characterized. In addition, the available therapeutic options are merely symptomatic and supportive, with [side effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/side-effect "Learn more about side effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as confusion, [dizziness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/dizziness "Learn more about dizziness from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), depression, constipation, and diarrhea [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0015). Progress towards effective disease-modifying therapies has proven challenging despite the wealth of knowledge on the molecular underpinnings of AD. For instance, several [clinical trials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/clinical-trial "Learn more about clinical trials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have failed to meet efficacy standards against Aβ production, accumulation, and toxicity. This put questions on the [amyloid beta](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amyloid-beta "Learn more about amyloid beta from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") hypothesis and advocates for additional treatment strategies. One of the very important and recently invented strategies, clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats/CRISPR-associated proteins 9 system (CRISPR/Cas9) gene editing, attracted attention for possible benefits in the management and [treatment of AD](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/treatment-of-alzheimers-disease "Learn more about treatment of AD from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This emerging technology is relatively straightforward, inexpensive, and precise, which has led to an increased interest in this technique for [neurodegenerative diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/degenerative-disease "Learn more about neurodegenerative diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (NDDs). This can be utilized as a direct treatment approach or may assist in establishing better [animal models](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/animal-model "Learn more about animal models from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that faithfully mimic human NDDs. Though, this technique has shown promise in other NDDs such as Huntington’s disease (HD) and Parkinson’s disease (PD). But, the potential of this technology in the management of AD has not been thoroughly discussed or documented elsewhere. Therefore, the purpose of this review was to examine the potential utility of CRISPR/Cas9 as a treatment option for AD by targeting specific genes, including those that cause [early onset AD](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/early-onset-alzheimers-disease "Learn more about early onset AD from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), as well as those that are significant risk factors for late-onset AD such as the [apolipoprotein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/apolipoprotein "Learn more about apolipoprotein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") E4 (APOE4) gene. This review also discusses various delivery systems that help in the proper and targeted delivery of CRISPR/Cas9 cargo in cells.

Alzheimer's disease at a glance
-------------------------------

AD is a ubiquitous form of dementia that affects the health of millions of individuals globally. Despite being known for over a century, numerous questions about its [pathophysiology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pathophysiology "Learn more about pathophysiology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are yet to be answered. The standard clinical characteristics of AD are displayed by diminished cognitive abilities, including memory, recognition, [judgment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/decision-making "Learn more about judgment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and problem solving [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0020), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0025), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0030). Studies on the [AD brain](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/encephalopathy "Learn more about AD brain from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") revealed neuropathological changes that represent the hallmarks of the condition, such as intracellular [neurofibrillary tangles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/neurofibrillary-tangle "Learn more about neurofibrillary tangles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (NFTs) comprising hyperphosphorylated Tau and accumulation of extracellular Aβ plaques [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0035), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0040), [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0045). In [early onset AD](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/early-onset-alzheimers-disease "Learn more about early onset AD from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), symptoms are exhibited in individuals aged between 30 and 65 years and is primarily [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), as observed in>92% cases reported [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0030), whereas in late onset AD, symptoms start after 65 years of age. As per a 2019 report, >5.8 million individuals in the United States (USA) alone have been diagnosed with AD, out of which 45% of subjects are reported in the 75–84 years group [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0050). The constantly increasing numbers of affected patients leads to projections of approximately 14 million in the USA by 2050 [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0055).

Mostly, the disease is believed to be due to external factors other than [genetic predisposition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetic-predisposition "Learn more about genetic predisposition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The amyloid hypothesis explains Aβ formation and aggregation in the brain. This hypothesis states that amyloid beta precursor protein (APP) undergoes [proteolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/protein-degradation "Learn more about proteolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") due to the concerted activities of α-, β-, and γ-secretases. An increase in β-secretase 1 (BACE1) activity is responsible for AD owing to aggregation of Aβ [monomers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/monomer "Learn more about monomers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") into [oligomers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/oligomer "Learn more about oligomers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and consequently the generation and deposition of Aβ plaques. Furthermore, BACE1-cleaved APP leads to the formation of a C99 fragment further cleaved by the γ-secretase to produce Aβ [monomers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/monomer "Learn more about monomers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Aβ40 and Aβ42. Interestingly, the α-secretase can also cleave APP at various sites, thus curtailing Aβ monomer generation ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#f0005)**A**) [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0060).

Schematic showing [Amyloid beta](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amyloid-beta "Learn more about Amyloid beta from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and Tau hypothesis that have been suggested to give an explanation for the most common characteristic hallmarks of [AD](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/alzheimers-disease "Learn more about AD from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). [APP](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/amyloid-precursor-protein "Learn more about APP from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), amyloid precursor protein; AICD, [APP](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amyloid-precursor-protein "Learn more about APP from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") intracellular domain; [NFTs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/neurofibrillary-tangle "Learn more about NFTs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), neurofibrillary tangles; [GSK](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/glycogen-synthase-kinase "Learn more about GSK from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), glycogen synthase kinase; CDK5, cyclin-dependent kinase 5; PP2A, [protein phosphatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/phosphoprotein-phosphatase "Learn more about protein phosphatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") 2A.

The amyloid hypothesis explains Aβ formation; similarly, NFT formation in AD brains is revealed by the popular Tau hypothesis. Tau, a well-known [microtubule associated protein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/microtubule-associated-protein "Learn more about microtubule associated protein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), plays a pivotal role in the formation and stabilization of the microtubule [cytoskeleton](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cytoskeleton "Learn more about cytoskeleton from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0065). It has been reported that out of six Tau [isoforms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/isoform "Learn more about isoforms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), 3R and 4R are prominent in adult human axons. Multiple phosphates and kinases have Tau as target. In the AD brain, Tau isoforms 3R and 4R can accumulate in hyperphosphorylated form and cause NFT formation in neuronal tissues, if present in neuronal cell axons and bodies, leading to Tau pathology. As recently suggested, Tau [oligomers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/oligomer "Learn more about oligomers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be neuropathology-mediating microstructures or potential molecular initiators in AD ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#f0005)**B**) [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0070). Furthermore, a possible molecular link between Aβ deposition and NFT formation has also been reported. The latter has been reported to inhibit neuronal viability, neuroplasticity, and altered [microtubule assembly](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/microtubule-assembly "Learn more about microtubule assembly from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), along with inhibition of mitochondrial transportation along microtubules. Hence, Tau [neurotoxicity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/neurotoxicity "Learn more about neurotoxicity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") may be driving an event downstream from Aβ polymerization, and be responsible for the [neurotoxicity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurotoxicity "Learn more about neurotoxicity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") caused by Tau [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0075). However, this hypothesis needs to be experimentally confirmed.

The introduction of the amyloid beta hypothesis provided directions for the development and testing of therapeutic agents for disease modification, which mutually enhance clearance of toxic peptides out of the brain and prevent Aβ formation [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123221001351#b0080). Unfortunately, >400 [clinical trials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/clinical-trial "Learn more about clinical trials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") conducted over the decade when the last drug for AD was approved have failed. The drug only offered temporary treatment for AD symptoms. enriched_points.expanded_content: CRISPR/Cas9 technology enables precise modifications to DNA, offering innovative solutions for correcting mutations associated with neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.
Recent advancements in stem cell research facilitate better modeling of neurodegenerative diseases, enhancing our understanding and enabling targeted gene therapies for patient-specific treatments.
Gene editing tailored to individual genetic profiles may lead to personalized therapies, effectively addressing the unique mutations and disease mechanisms in neurodegenerative patients.
